U.S. Strategic Oil Releases Buy Time - But Not Much More

Since the start of the war with Iran and effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz, U.S. petroleum inventories have fallen at some of the fastest rates on record, acting a shock absorber to avert fuel shortages and limit price rises.

The United States has become the supplier of last resort, accelerating exports of crude and refined products to the rest of the world, making up for some of the oil lost from the Persian Gulf.

The government owned Strategic Petroleum Reserve has functioned as intended to forestall panic buying and hoarding, minimise the impact on the national economy, and allow additional time for diplomatic and military planning.  

But the rate of inventory depletion is unsustainable, which explains the increasing urgency to find a way to re-open the Strait through negotiations or the provision of military protection to tankers transiting the waterway ...

Goldman Slashes 2027 Brent Outlook to $80

Goldman Sachs cut its average 2027 Brent crude forecast to $80/bbl, from $85, pointing to robust non-OPEC supply growth and structural demand weakness, led by China’s EV push and efficiency gains.

It held Q4 2026 at $90/bbl, noting the Hormuz disruption’s price impact has been milder than expected thanks to softer demand.

Goldman is doubling down on the fundamentals over geopolitics thesis.

Even with ongoing Strait risks, they see supply flooding in and demand softening enough to cap longer-term prices.

Goldman Sachs just cut its 2027 Brent crude estimate to $80 per barrel.

The revision is based on two factors: stronger supply from non-OPEC producers and structurally weaker demand from China.

Goldman estimates that over 10% of China's demand decline becomes permanent as the country diversifies its energy mix away from oil.

This matters because China has been the marginal buyer driving global oil demand growth for two decades. A structural shift changes the long-term calculus for crude.

Brent recently dropped 6.2% in a single session. WTI fell 5.9%. These moves may reflect the market repricing not just short-term supply concerns, but longer-term demand uncertainty.

The contrast is notable: short-term volatility is getting headlines, but the bigger story is a major bank revising its multi-year outlook lower based on permanent demand destruction.

Worth watching whether other banks follow with similar revisions.

Short-term volatility, but the real story is structural demand - watch China.

...anything that gets shipped, because $90 oil doesn't stay at the refinery. It moves through everything.

Goldman isn't panicking and isn't revising down. That's the signal. In a week where oil has been pulled in every direction by trade war fears and demand uncertainty, the bank looked at all of it and kept its number exactly where it was.

At $90 a barrel, petrol prices stay elevated. Not crisis-level, but well above the sub-$70 lows we saw earlier this year. The relief at the pump that drivers briefly felt doesn't fully come back.

The hidden pressure is on industries that run on diesel and freight. Supermarkets, logistics companies, manufacturers. Their input costs stay high. That feeds into prices on the shelf, quietly, over months.

Goldman's refusal to cut the forecast is essentially a bet that the world keeps consuming oil faster than it can be replaced by alternatives. That bet has been right for two years running.

The U.S.–Iran conflict pushed Brent crude from roughly $70/barrel in February to peaks above $120/barrel in March–April — a rise of over 70% at the peak. Prices later eased to around $90–95/barrel.

Cainhoy Peninsula, South Carolina. From the Cainhoy dock.

The outgoing tide runs dark past the pilings while the marsh settles into night. A light breeze moves through the Spartina and the Wando carries what it must. The fixed perch holds.

The Strait remains the live test. Iranian drones continue probing commercial traffic and U.S. defenses even as diplomatic theater claims progress on the MOU. U.S. forces have shot down multiple one-way attack drones in recent hours.  

The MOU itself stays un-signed. Pakistan claims a final text exists. Araghchi says it has never been closer and could be signed remotely in coming days. Iranian hardline and IRGC messaging quickly rejected key leaked terms on Hormuz management and nuclear sequencing.  

The central question is who we are dealing with. Authority inside Iran remains fragmented. The IRGC continues to operate as the dominant occupying force, shaping what any leadership can accept without appearing to surrender. Surface statements and internal veto power are not aligned.  

Lowcountry Lesson
The Wando does not negotiate with the tide. It carries what upstream decisions send and shows plainly what settles against the pilings. Position belongs to the one who stays fixed long enough to separate signal from drift.

Grateful for every watcher holding position.  

DYOR. NFA.  

Throne steady. Kingdom vigilant. Unbroken.

Fujairah (UAE) documented history of oil-related pollution in its coastal waters, which aligns with what you’ve observed.
Fujairah sits on the Gulf of Oman and hosts one of the world’s largest oil storage, bunkering (ship refueling), and export terminals. This brings heavy tanker traffic, offshore anchoring, and related activities, making oil pollution a recurring issue.
Main Causes
•Illegal dumping and operational discharges — Tankers often dump oily ballast water or sludge. Reports indicate this happens roughly once a month on average along Fujairah’s coast.40
•Accidental spills — Collisions, leaks from terminals, or incidents near ports. Examples include:
◦A 2025 spill near the port affecting hotel beaches (e.g., near Novotel, Al Bahar, Ibis), with fuel oil smells and swimming bans.33
◦Multiple incidents in recent years, including ones hitting Al Aqah/Dibba areas and near Snoopy Island (quickly cleaned in some cases).39
◦Historical major events, like the 1994 SEKI spill (16,000 tonnes off Fujairah).32
•Regional factors — High shipping volume through the Strait of Hormuz and Gulf of Oman, plus occasional sabotage or conflict-related incidents adding risk.35
Authorities (Fujairah Environment Authority) respond with cleanup teams, beach closures, and investigations, but the problem persists due to the area’s industrial role.67
Other Pollutants
Oil/tar is the most visible and commonly reported issue. Broader water quality data for Fujairah’s marine/coastal areas is limited in public sources compared to places like Dubai or Abu Dhabi, but the heavy hydrocarbon activity suggests potential for related contaminants (e.g., hydrocarbons, heavy metals from shipping). No major widespread reports of other severe pollutants (like industrial chemicals or sewage) dominate headlines, but the oil focus overshadows them.20
Beaches can look clean after cleanups, but tar balls or slicks often reappear, especially after tanker activity. Visitors and locals frequently report this on forums and reviews.60
Practical Advice
•Check recent local news or the Fujairah Environment Authority for current beach status before swimming.
•Affected areas often include spots near the port, Al Aqah, Dibba, and hotel beaches — east coast spots farther from the main terminal may be better.
•The UAE monitors and responds, but the volume of shipping makes zero pollution unrealistic.
In short, your experience is common and tied to Fujairah’s role as a major oil hub. It’s not the cleanest marine environment in the UAE for that reason.

And now they want to add an additional pipeline and bunkers to Fujairah.

The SPR (Strategic Petroleum Reserve) has bought time, not barrels for refiners. US commercial crude inventories drew more than 10 million barrels in the latest EIA week — one of the sharpest weekly draws on record — on top of six prior consecutive weeks of above-estimate declines. Diplomatic resolution doesn't flip that overnight; tanker re-routing back through Hormuz adds weeks of physical lag. The physical market is tighter now than it was before the SPR started releasing.

no offense, but the report was not great. 

You guessed short term direction right, but I find the logic very unconvincing. Also do not like strawman arguments (like saying that "This is the part of the story we think most people are missing. When the Strait of Hormuz closed, the assumption was that the oil stopped flowing." - absolutely nobody serious is missing the rerouting). 

Why is Andurand there? I am also often wondering what the hell they are doing, but his fund performance is completely unconnected with the topic. Also feels like a bit of schadenfreude.

China demand - imo wrong reasons for the drop ... 
There aren't a ton of large weakness signs, main adjustment components are pause in stockpiling / refinery run cuts (unclear if sustainable - most likely not) / inventory draws.

Overall no estimate on the size of the hole / inventory draws / no details on demand destruction estimates. 
You cannot really say - ok - we will deal with 13mbd production shut in - need to show how, because it is a big number.

No details on products (where a bulk of the draws are) or a forward trajectory of US crude inventory ...

"Moving from Middle Eastern oil" - if there is a gap everyone cannot move at the same time.

I frankly even decided not to comment back then but I think this report needs much more work. I actually would love to see a good bearish or neutral thesis much more than yet another bullish take, but it was not it.

The bearish structural case is mostly right on demand, but the supply side has a deferred cliff that the BNEF model may be underpricing. Non-OPEC+ growth in 2026 is running at ~1.1 mb/d — Brazil, Guyana, Argentina carrying the load — but upstream capex is falling for a second straight year under sub-$60 oil. Projects being sanctioned today come online 2028-2030. If demand doesn't crater fast enough, the supply growth story reverses exactly when Middle East normalization ends. The bearish window is real; the duration is shorter than consensus implies.

1.  핵심 내용 
​상황: 전 세계에서 최소 121척의 **'비어 있는 유조선(Empty Oil Tankers)'**들이 기름을 채우기 위해 일제히 미국으로 향하고 있다는 선박 항로 추적 데이터(Marine Traffic) 뉴스입니다.
​규모: 그중 68척은 한 번에 무려 200만 배럴의 원유를 실을 수 있는 초대형 원유운반선(VLCC)입니다.
​의미: "이제 우리는 세계의 주유소가 되었습니다!"라는 대사처럼, 과거 석유를 사 오던 미국이 이제는 전 세계 배들이 기름을 받으러 줄을 서는 세계 최대의 석유 공급국이 되었다는 것을 시각적으로 증명하는 자료입니다.

​2. 왜 '기름이 꽉 찬 배'가 아니라 '빈 배'가 갈까요?
​일반적으로 중동이나 러시아에서 석유를 수입할 때는 기름이 가득 찬 유조선이 미국으로 들어와야 합니다. 하지만 지금은 반대로 텅 빈 유조선들이 미국으로 떼를 지어 가고 있습니다.
즉, 전 세계 국가들이 "미국산 석유를 사서 자국으로 가져가려고" 배를 보낸 상황입니다. 미국의 석유 수출 수요가 어마어마하게 폭발했다는 증거입니다.
​3. 글로벌 시장 및 정치적 배경 분석
​미국의 에너지 패권 장악: 앞선 뉴스에서 미국이 사우디아라비아와 러시아를 제치고 세계 1위 석유 수출국이 되었다는 소식과 정확히 맞물리는 현상입니다.
​트럼프 행정부의 성과 과시: 트럼프 대통령의 "조직적인 규제 완화"와 "전폭적인 시추 허용(Drill, Baby, Drill)" 정책이 전 세계 유조선들을 미국 국경으로 불러 모으는 결과를 냈다고 지지층들이 환호하는 포인트입니다.
​달러화 및 공급망 영향: 전 세계 배들이 미국에 와서 기름을 사 가려면 결국 달러를 써야 하므로, 미국의 달러 패권이 강해지고 글로벌 에너지 가격 통제권도 사우디(OPEC)나 러시아가 아닌 미국이 쥐게 됨을 뜻합니다.
​
요약:
"전 세계 유조선들이 미국산 석유를 수입해 가려고 빈 통 들고 줄을 서기 시작했으며, 이로써 미국이 글로벌 에너지 시장의 지배자('세계의 주유소')가 되었음을 보여주는 시황 분석입니다."
